title: Address to the 79th Session of the United Nations General Assembly
occasion: 79th Session of the United Nations General Assembly

paragraphs.text: Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen. I come before this Assembly at a time of profound consequence for Israel, for the Middle East, and for the entire world. Nearly one year ago, on October 7th, the State of Israel suffered the worst massacre of Jewish people since the Holocaust. Fifteen hundred terrorists breached our border and murdered 1,200 people in a single day. They butchered families in their homes. They slaughtered young people at a music festival. They committed unspeakable atrocities.

I hold up before this Assembly two maps. The first is a map of the blessing — the Abraham Accords and the expanding circle of peace between Israel and its Arab neighbors. The second is a map of the curse — Iran’s axis of terror that stretches from Tehran through Baghdad, Damascus, Beirut, Sanaa, and Gaza. On October 7th, Iran’s proxies tried to destroy the map of blessing. They will not succeed.

Israel did not start this war. We did not seek this war. But we will finish it. Hamas declared war on Israel, and Israel will ensure that Hamas can never again threaten our citizens. This is not merely a matter of deterrence. It is a matter of survival. No country in this hall would tolerate what Israel endured on October 7th, and no country would be expected to.

I have heard the calls in this Assembly for a ceasefire. Israel wants peace. Israel has always wanted peace. But peace cannot mean returning to the status quo of October 6th. It cannot mean allowing a terrorist army to rebuild on our border, to rearm, and to plan the next massacre. That is not peace. That is a ticking time bomb. And Israel will not accept it.

Let me be clear about the civilian casualties in Gaza, because I know that many in this hall focus on this above all else. Every civilian death is a tragedy. Israel does more to prevent civilian casualties than any army in history facing similar circumstances. We drop leaflets, we make phone calls, we send text messages warning civilians to evacuate areas of military operations. Hamas, by contrast, deliberately places its military infrastructure inside hospitals, schools, and mosques. Hamas wants civilian casualties because it knows that the world will blame Israel.

The real obstacle to peace in the Middle East has a name: Iran. The regime in Tehran is the world’s leading state sponsor of terrorism. It funds Hamas. It arms Hezbollah with over 150,000 rockets and missiles aimed at Israeli cities. It supports the Houthis who attack international shipping. It provides weapons and training to militias in Iraq and Syria. Iran’s tentacles of terror threaten not only Israel but every nation in this region and beyond.

And let there be no illusion about Iran’s nuclear ambitions. The Iranian regime has enriched uranium to 60 percent purity, a short technical step from weapons-grade material. It has restricted access for international inspectors. It has expanded its centrifuge capacity far beyond any civilian energy need. The world cannot afford a nuclear-armed Iran. It would trigger a nuclear arms race in the most volatile region on earth and place the world’s most dangerous weapons in the hands of the world’s most dangerous regime.

Israel stands ready to defend itself, by itself, against any threat. We have demonstrated this throughout our history. But I want to emphasize: Israel’s fight against Iran’s axis of terror is not Israel’s fight alone. When we confront Hamas in Gaza, when we defend against Hezbollah in the north, we are defending civilization against barbarism. We are defending the values that this institution was founded to protect.

The Abraham Accords represent the most significant breakthrough for peace in the Middle East in a generation. Israel has normalized relations with the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain, Morocco, and Sudan. Before October 7th, we were on the verge of a historic agreement with Saudi Arabia that would have transformed the entire region. I believe that this vision of peace is still within reach. The forces of moderation in the Arab world understand that Israel is not their enemy. Iran is.

To those in this Assembly who have recognized a Palestinian state in recent months, I say this: you have not advanced peace. You have rewarded terrorism. You have sent a message that the path to statehood runs through massacre and hostage-taking. Palestinian statehood, if it is to come, must be the result of direct negotiations, not of unilateral declarations driven by political pressure in the aftermath of a terrorist attack on Israeli civilians.

I want to address the hostages. More than one hundred Israelis and foreign nationals remain in Hamas captivity in Gaza, enduring conditions that defy description. Their families live in agony every single day. I call on every member of this Assembly: demand the immediate and unconditional release of all hostages. This is not a political issue. It is a humanitarian imperative of the most basic kind.

UNRWA, the United Nations agency for Palestinian refugees, has been thoroughly compromised. We have provided evidence that UNRWA employees participated in the October 7th massacre. UNRWA facilities were used to store weapons and as entrances to Hamas tunnel networks. An organization that was infiltrated by a terrorist group cannot continue to operate as though nothing has happened. The international community must find alternative mechanisms to deliver humanitarian assistance.

The threats facing Israel on our northern border have escalated dramatically. Hezbollah has fired thousands of rockets at Israeli communities, forcing the evacuation of tens of thousands of Israeli citizens from their homes. Israel will do whatever is necessary to ensure that our citizens can return safely to their homes in the north. We will not accept a permanent state of displacement for our people imposed by an Iranian proxy army.

Israel is a nation of innovation and resilience. Even as we fight on multiple fronts, our economy continues to generate breakthrough technologies in artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, water technology, and agricultural science. We are a startup nation that has turned adversity into advantage. The same spirit that built a thriving democracy in the most challenging neighborhood on earth will sustain us through this crisis as well.

I want to speak directly to the people of Iran. Your fight is not with Israel. Our fight is not with you. It is with your oppressive regime that squanders your resources on foreign wars and terrorist proxies while your own people suffer. The day will come when the great Iranian people are free, and on that day, Israel and Iran can be allies once again, as we were before the 1979 revolution.

History has taught the Jewish people an indelible lesson: when someone threatens to destroy you, take them seriously. We have taken the threats of Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iran seriously. We have acted accordingly. And we will continue to act to protect our people with every means at our disposal. This is not a choice. It is a sacred obligation.

I say to this Assembly: Israel seeks a future of peace and prosperity for the entire region. We are prepared to work with any nation or any leader who shares this vision. But we will never compromise on our security, and we will never apologize for defending our people. The curse of October 7th will be answered with the blessing of a safer, more peaceful Middle East. Thank you.
